flashcards.front: What is a graph in discrete mathematics?
flashcards.back: A graph G = (V, E) is a mathematical structure consisting of a finite set of vertices (V) and a set of edges (E) that connect pairs of vertices.
A simple graph is an undirected graph with no self-loops (edges from a vertex to itself) and no multiple edges (parallel edges between the same pair of vertices).

flashcards.front: What is the difference between a directed and undirected graph?
flashcards.back: In an undirected graph, edges have no direction (bidirectional); in a directed graph (digraph), each edge has a specific direction from one vertex to another.

flashcards.front: Define the degree of a vertex.
flashcards.back: The degree of a vertex is the number of edges incident to it. In directed graphs, outdegree (edges leaving) and indegree (edges entering) are distinguished.
A complete graph Kn has n vertices where every pair of distinct vertices is connected by a unique edge. It has n(n-1)/2 edges.
A regular graph is a graph where all vertices have the same degree. A k-regular graph has all vertices of degree k.
A bipartite graph's vertices can be divided into two disjoint sets such that every edge connects a vertex from one set to a vertex in the other set.
A weighted graph assigns a numerical weight (cost, distance, capacity) to each edge. These weights represent quantities like distance, time, or cost.
An adjacency matrix is an n×n matrix where entry a[i][j] = 1 if there's an edge between vertices i and j, else 0. For weighted graphs, it stores the weight values.
An adjacency list represents a graph by storing for each vertex, a list of its neighboring vertices. It's space-efficient for sparse graphs.
Two graphs are isomorphic if there's a one-to-one correspondence between their vertices that preserves the adjacency relationship (edges).

flashcards.front: What is a path in a graph?
flashcards.back: A path is a sequence of vertices where each consecutive pair is connected by an edge. The length equals the number of edges traversed.

flashcards.front: Define a cycle in graph theory.
flashcards.back: A cycle is a path that starts and ends at the same vertex without repeating any edge or vertex (except the starting/ending vertex).
A graph is connected if there's a path between every pair of vertices. In directed graphs, strong connectivity requires paths in both directions.
A spanning tree is a subgraph that is a tree containing all vertices of the original graph with minimum possible number of edges (n-1 edges).
